title: Former Penn State track and field standout Sincere Rhea chases new finish line on Love Island
author: Elliott Inscho | Sports Editor
description:
Sincere Rhea made a name for himself when he won a Big Ten 60-meter hurdles championship at Penn State in 2021, but the biggest break of his career has ended up coming off the track.

The Cape May, New Jersey, native is now playing a prominent role in the newest season of Love Island, a reality TV show that has taken the internet by storm in recent years.

When he stepped foot into the Fijian villa on Tuesday, Rhea joined a crew of ten islanders competing for a grand prize of $100,000. Love Island contestants win through public support, as viewers cast votes on the show’s app for their favorites, and the couple with the most votes at the end of the 6-week season wins.

Love Island has given its stars a fast-track to fame in the past, but Rhea already has some blazing speed.

A St. Augustine Prep product, the sprinter initially committed to Penn State in September 2018. In his first two seasons as a Nittany Lion, he claimed bronze medals at the 2019 and 2020 Big Ten Championships. In the process, he recorded a mark of 7.76 in the 60-meter hurdles that sits at second-best in program history.

In 2021, Rhea secured the aforementioned Big Ten title with a time of 7.80. He also participated in the outdoor 110-meter hurdles, advancing to the NCAA East Preliminary Round where he placed 30th with a time of 14.01.

After claiming the championship, Rhea transferred to Miami. He continued to favor the 60-meter hurdles as a Hurricane, winning the event at the 2023 Louisville Cardinal Classic and posting a then-career best 7.73 at the Texas Tech Red Raider Open that places third all-time in Miami history.

To finish off his collegiate career, Rhea transferred again, this time to Texas Tech. During his time as a Red Raider, he set the quickest time of his 60-meter hurdles career with a 7.64 at the Stan Scott Invite.

He closed his time at Texas Tech and finished his track career in the NCAA West First Round, where he posted a time of 13.72 in the 110-meter hurdles.

Season 8 of Love Island debuted on Tuesday and can be streamed on Peacock. New episodes will drop daily for the first week of the season, before the show shifts to a five episode per week schedule.

title: Staff predictions | What will Penn State be rated in College Football 27?
author: The Daily Collegian football staff
description:
The third edition of the revamped EA College Football game is about a month away. But unlike the last two seasons, EA has a bit of an unknown at its hands with Penn State.

At release last year, the Nittany Lions were given an 88 overall with their offense rated a 91 and their defense at a 94. Those numbers will surely look a bit different this year, as Penn State isn’t surrounded with national championship-level expectations and has a brand new head coach in Matt Campbell.

Although it’s hard to tell how good Penn State will be this season, our staff took a crack at predicting the Nittany Lions’ overall ratings ahead of the game’s release on July 9.

Andrew Deal

Offense: 86

Penn State’s offense will still be highly rated despite an almost completely fresh look. The final update on ratings from last season has the Nittany Lions’ offense at an 83 overall, which significantly dropped due to its performance in 2025.

Penn State brings a plethora of Iowa State players to the offensive side of the ball, an offense that held an 85 overall rating in College Football 26, and should add to some of the returning Nittany Lions for a boosted rating.

While expectations are high for a potential young star in Koby Howard, his rating likely won’t reflect that of a future top receiving threat. The obvious top players rated will be quarterback Rocco Becht, tight end Benjamin Brahmer and running back Carson Hansen, all who were 86 overall or higher in the most recent roster update.

The offensive line should be filled with highly rated starters, with the expectation that Anthony Donkoh gets a bit of a boost for his consistency on the field, while the young tackles might get a bigger jump for being first-year starters.

The offense will be lower rated than last year’s team due to the difference in flashy names, but it has plenty of room for growth throughout the season, barring the on-field play of a newly formed team.

Defense: 87

On the other hand, the defense will be around the same overall as last year’s game. It has plenty of highly rated returning names to the Nittany Lions, in addition to some big time ratings boosts from transfers.

Jeremiah Cooper was a 90 overall at the end of the season, and with a position change back to safety, his rating could get boosted higher due to his 93 speed in the game.

Tony Rojas will lead a star-studded linebacker room in the game, as he finished last year as an 88 overall, while the Iowa State transfer linebackers will compliment him well in the 80s.

The defensive line is where things get interesting. Both edge rushers have high upside, but weren’t rated high last year. Max Granville finished as a 74 overall while Yvan Kemajou ended the year with a 72 overall. Both players are expected to make a major impact this season, but will likely cause a hit to the defensive rating.

Overall: 86

Andrew Mercer

Offense: 86

Penn State’s offense will look entirely different compared to last season with lots of Iowa State transfers, so I’d expect the ratings to be closer to how the Cyclones were in last year’s game.

Rocco Becht labored through shoulder injuries throughout most of his 2025 campaign, but was given an 88 overall. After undergoing surgery on his torn labrum, Becht should be in the spot he wants to be in physically, but I’d wager that EA may be hesitant to alter his rating at launch.

Penn State will likely have yet another strong running back tandem in Carson Hansen and James Peoples. They won’t be as highly rated as Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen were, but Peoples is due for a boost from an 85 to potentially match Hansen’s 88 from last year.

The offensive line is where things start to get complicated. Only one of Penn State’s projected starters — Trevor Buhr — had a rating surpass 80 overall, but Malachi Goodman should make a leap this season as he presumably grows into the starting left tackle role.

I believe it will be Cooper Cousins, however, that sees the biggest jump in rating among the offensive linemen. He’s bounced around different roles for the past two seasons, earning a 77 overall in last year’s game, but he appears to have a firmer slot at right guard this season, resulting in a potential rise to the low 80s.

Defense: 88

This year, Penn State has a highly fluctuating roster when it comes to EA’s ratings. While the Nittany Lions are very top heavy with Jeremiah Cooper and Tony Rojas, there are a good deal of players in the low 80s and high 70s. If EA gives the lower rated players a boost, gamers should expect to see Penn State’s overall defense in the high 80s.

Audavion Collins and Zion Tracy, two returners from last year, were rated in the mid 70s. Collins proved his worth throughout the season and is likely due for a jump. Tracy, formerly at a 74 overall, should be ready for a gain, since he started nine out of 11 games in 2025.

The safety room, however, is all Cyclone transfers. Marcus Neal Jr. — formerly an 81 overall — at strong safety is projected to make a big leap in the ratings after making 77 tackles, two interceptions and a forced fumble last season.

Then there’s Cooper, the lone current 90 overall, who will be returning to safety after spending the games prior to his season-ending injury as a cornerback. He’ll give the Nittany Lions higher numbers on defense.

The only other potential 90 overall that I’d keep an eye out for is Rojas. Like Cooper, he suffered a season-ending injury when he was supposed to make huge leaps. While EA may hesitate to bump him up, it’s possible for him to improve to the low 90s.

Overall: 87

Lexie Linderman

Offense: 85

In last year’s version of this game, Iowa State’s offense was rated an 85, and with the entire core of that group — quarterback Rocco Becht, running back Carson Hansen, tight end Benjamin Brahmer and wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen — transferring to Penn State, I think that number stays the same at their new destination.

The only big difference is the offensive line, which currently features three returnees and two transfers. Although their ceiling is high, it's currently unknown how that group will actually perform, so EA won’t give the offensive rating a bump because of that.

There are some other players on the Nittany Lions’ offense that will contribute to this rating, such as running back James Peoples, wide receiver Koby Howard and tight end Andrew Rappleyea. All three, however, haven’t showcased enough in their college careers so far to warrant a higher offensive ranking.

I’d expect Becht and Brahmer to be the highest-rated guys for Penn State on this side of the ball. Becht was rated 88 last season, a number that might not see much movement in either direction after an average year, and Brahmer was given an 86 in the previous edition of the game.

Defense: 88

With another new defensive coordinator at Penn State and plenty of transfers, it’s hard to pinpoint where exactly EA will rate this group.

The Nittany Lions have a long history of fielding elite defenses, so I wouldn’t be surprised if they get a rankings bump just because of that. Even so, Penn State has plenty of talent on the defensive side of the ball that would warrant an 88 overall.

Marcus Neal Jr. and Jeremiah Cooper will surely be given the highest ratings, with Cooper expected to land in the high 80s or low 90s. Neal will likely be lower than that in the low to mid 80s, but the Iowa State transfers are two of the more proven players in Penn State’s defensive backfield.

The others are Audavion Collins and Zion Tracy. Collins finished last season with a 76 overall rating, while Tracy was a 74. Those two numbers should see slight increases to start the year, while young cornerback Daryus Dixson should build on his 78 overall rating to end 2025.

As for the defensive line, the veteran defensive tackles should hover around the high 70s and low 80s like they have prior, while the young edge rushers might have lower rankings considering most are unproven, resulting in a 88 overall rating for the Nittany Lions’ defense.

Overall: 85

Noah Aberegg

Offense: 87

Penn State will look very different this year with a new quarterback and an almost entirely new arsenal of weapons.

Rocco Becht and Carson Hansen are expected to be two of the highest players on the team, as the duo finished at 88 overall last season.

However, Hansen will also be challenged by James Peoples in the backfield, who was an 85 overall despite being listed as the No. 3 back on Ohio State’s roster. He’ll be complemented by another likely mid-80 overall back in Quinton Martin Jr., who was ranked 81 overall last season.

Wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen are expected to be the top two targets. Their ratings, as well as fellow wideout Koby Howard and tight ends Benjamin Brahamer and Andrew Rappleyea, will likely dictate Penn State’s offensive rating.

Defense: 90

The Nittany Lions’ defense boasts a handful of key defensive returners, including Tony Rojas, Audavion Collins and Zion Tracy. While many new faces will be introduced throughout the starting lineup, most will continue to keep Penn State as one of the best defenses in the game.

While those three will likely be the highest-ranked returners to Happy Valley, Jeremiah Cooper, Caleb Bacon and Keanu Williams have all made their cases to be higher rated players as well.

Of those six, Cooper is the only player on Penn State’s 2026 squad who was ranked 90 or higher last year, but that could very well change. Despite Rojas’ season-ending injury, he was rated 88 overall and saw a pair of sacks and 25 tackles through four games, which could result in a ratings boost.

Tracy will be the starting nickel once again this season, and after recording 32 tackles and a forced fumble last season, he could also see his rating increase from the 74 overall he had when College Football 26 first dropped.

Overall: 88

title: ‘I'm excited to be here’ | Dwayne Stephens settles in as new AC for Penn State men’s basketball
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
While Dwayne Stephens was hired as an assistant coach for Penn State in May, he’s been around the program before.

He faced the Nittany Lions during his playing days at Michigan State from 1989 to 1993, and again as an assistant on the Spartans from 2003 to 2022. His brother, Jarrett, was a standout player for Penn State in the late 1990s as well.

Now, Stephens is bringing a wealth of experience to Mike Rhoades’ staff.

Rhoades said Stephens has already made a “great positive impact” as he settles into Happy Valley.

“I just appreciate the opportunity to be here,” Stephens said on Friday. “Obviously, this is a special place to my brother, and I can understand why. After being here for a few weeks now, it's a unique place like nowhere that I've ever seen in terms of a true college town, and I can see just the pride that everyone takes in supporting the university and Penn State athletics, and I'm excited to be here.”

Stephens worked with Michigan State head coach Tom Izzo for 19 seasons, including 11 years as associate head coach. During his time there, the Spartans made 20 NCAA Tournaments and six Final Fours in addition to winning six Big Ten championships and four Big Ten Tournament titles.

He played a role in developing NBA-level talent for Michigan State, such as Draymond Green, Miles Bridges and Jaren Jackson Jr. This experience allowed him to become known as one of the nation’s top recruiting and player development coaches, according to Penn State’s news release from when he was hired.

“That’s a flagship program that he was at for a long time, was a part of the teams that went to the pinnacle of college basketball,” Rhoades said Friday. “I think that's a valuable resource for us.”

Prior to joining the Nittany Lions, Stephens was the head coach at Western Michigan from 2022 to 2026. The Broncos went 42-84 when he was at the helm of the program, which led to the team parting ways with him earlier this year.

Still, Stephens has four years of head coaching experience, giving him a unique way to contribute to Penn State’s staff.

“I think it gives you a totally different perspective,” Stephens said. “I can be a much better assistant for coach Rhoades, understanding what he's going through on a day in, day out basis, hopefully being able to help him with some of the headaches that come with the job when you're in that top seat.”

With recruiting being one of Stephens’ specialties, Rhoades said he’s all about relationships and helping young players move forward. He mentioned he’s gotten to know Stephens for the past few years, and that Penn State would be a great fit for him and the team.

Stephens said NIL and the transfer portal have changed the recruiting world in recent years, so he won’t lock into a certain area for recruiting. He said he’ll go wherever to recruit players that fit what the team is looking for.

As Stephens begins his time at Penn State to recruit and develop players, he’s said he’s happy to be a part of Rhoades’ staff. However, he did mention in a statement when he was hired that there’s one person more excited about this opportunity than him: his brother.

“It's great just seeing him at my alma mater,” Jarrett said on Friday. “When I first found out, I was super excited. Hopefully he can bring something special to me for an already special program, and just to see him in the colors is great.”

title: What to bring to your Penn State dorm room
author: Alli Satterfield | News Editor
description:
I’m a resident assistant on campus, so I’m familiar with the emotions of transitioning college students. But also, the emotions of their parents.

A lot of parents step into dorm buildings not knowing what to expect, and they’re nervous to send their babies into this unknown land alone. But never fear; everything will work out in the end, especially if you’re prepared.

Here are some of the dorm essentials new students should definitely look into.

An ottoman/step stool

Dorm rooms are small, obviously, and they may be smaller than some students are used to, especially in terms of storage space. Ottomans are a great way to incorporate some extra storage into a dorm room, with a hint of practicality too.

A lot, if not all, of the Penn State dormitory beds are lofted, so ottomans can be a good way to have two uses for one purchase. They can hold your things while also helping you crawl into bed.

Even if you don’t need the extra storage, I would recommend a step stool so you don’t have to do acrobatics to get into your bed.

Fans

I’d also recommend getting a fan (or two) for your living space.

When I first moved in, I questioned whether or not I would actually need a fan at Penn State. It gets so cold during the winter that I assumed I wouldn’t want to make my space any colder. But after two years here, I’m so glad my dad encouraged me to keep it “just in case.”

Since dorm rooms are a little bit smaller, they keep heat in very well. Even in the dead of winter, I had my fan going. Now, with so many options online, there are easy ways to adjust speed and intensity, so fans are kind of a no-brainer.

Command strips

Decorations are a simple thing that can help make a dorm room feel more like home.

Although it can be difficult to decorate because of the restrictions in the dorms (you aren’t allowed to hang anything or decorate in a way that may damage or alter the paint or integrity of the room), command strips can be a life-saver.

They attach to the wall sturdily and can be removed with little-to-no residue. They also come in a variety of shapes and sizes, so they’re helpful to hang almost anything.

A comfier chair

There’ll be a chair paired with your desk when you arrive at your PSU dorm room. But if you spend a lot of time working at your desk, it may not be the comfiest option. They’re typically wooden chairs or small, plastic rollers.

I’m so grateful I got something for myself with a little bit of cushion and a lot more lumbar support. It’s also a great way to pick out a piece of furniture that feels more like you.

The cleaning works.

A lot of parents think going away to college is like going for a school year-long hotel stay — which couldn’t be further from the truth.

It’s important that you remember to pack anything you would use at home. Bring laundry detergent (liquid or pods), towels, linens, brooms and more, because it won’t be here waiting for you.

Although packing those essentials is crucial, there are some items that deserve to be left behind. The printer, steamer and mound of throw pillows Pinterest told you are an absolute must can probably stay at home.

And one more piece of advice: you don’t need to bring your whole closet. It won’t fit, and it’ll make moving all of this stuff out ten times harder.

Have a great NSO and an even better freshman year, new Penn Staters.

title: Beginner’s guide to must-know Penn State songs
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
So you’re a new student at Penn State on this beautiful campus.

Whether you’re on Frat Row (you’ll learn), the bars (you’ll be old enough soon) or your first White Out game, there are some songs that you’ll hear all over the place, and trust me when I say that you don’t want to be the lone one out who doesn’t know the words — we’ve all been there.

Penn State Alma Mater

Please, please, please, learn the words. They’re not hard to memorize. Beaver Stadium, Bryce Jordan Center, Pegula Ice Arena and virtually everywhere else around campus display the words for your convenience.

There was a version of the song popular among students back in the nineties that replaced the existing verses with one of their own: “we don’t know the bleep words.” You can guess what their expletive of choice was.

By learning this song — hopefully before your fourth year — you’ll immediately feel more connected to the school and those around you. Plus, you get to lock arms with your friends while singing it. What’s better than that?

Mo Bamba - Sheck Wes

Ahh, the name alone conjures up memories of that 2019 White Out game against Michigan (Sean Clifford, they could never make me hate you).

Whether it’s a huge third down against a tough division rival or an opponent taking a timeout on the first play of the game, you’re bound to hear this jam inside Beaver Stadium. You already know it, so prepare to hear it even more.

Seven Nation Army - The White Stripes

Similar to the last entry on my list, you know this one. You’ve heard it a thousand times. And guess what? The folks at Beaver Stadium play the heck out of this one, too. The Blue Band also plays it frequently, so there’s no escaping this White Stripes classic when you’re at Penn State.

Not a fan of the garage rock revival genre? Maybe think about attending a different school. It’s not too late. You won’t be able to get Peachy Paterno anywhere else, though. Wink wink.

Badlands - Bruce Springsteen

As a huge fan of The Boss, I had to include this.

This absolute classic usually plays at least once per football game, usually around the end of the third quarter or during a timeout. I look around at fellow students during these moments to see that many around me don’t know this gem, so familiarize yourself with Springsteen’s gravelly vocals.

Heck, you might even get into the rest of his discography, which I certainly recommend.

Sweet Caroline - Neil Diamond

Bum-bum-bum. You know it. I know you know it. We all know it’s overplayed, but it’s still a Beaver Stadium classic that usually plays during the fourth quarter.

Neil Diamond’s 1969 classic has been a staple at Red Sox games forever, but the student section at Beaver Stadium sure goes wild when this one comes on.

There’s a certain in-state school that Penn State students aren’t huge fans of, so maybe learn the alternative version of this song, too, if you know what I mean.

Unwritten - Natasha Bedingfield

Oh, to be a teenage girl in the early 2000’s. That’s how this song makes me feel.

You’ll hear this gem every now and then and what a treat it is when you do. Thinking about seeing everyone dancing with their friends in the stands to Natasha Bedingfield’s lovely voice makes me want to be in Beaver Stadium right now, eating a chicken basket.

In the Air Tonight - Phil Collins

My mind goes straight to Mike Tyson punching Zach Galifianakis in The Hangover when I hear this song.

Aside from that, I think of the White Out game, and seeing James Franklin lock arms with his players coming out of the tunnel and onto the field. Who knows what the Matt Campbell era will bring? I hope we hear this song at Beaver Stadium for years to come.

Hey! Baby - Bruce Channel

Without fail, the Blue Band plays this song at the start of the fourth quarter every Saturday, ushering the crowd to join in and sing. The lyrics are as simple as they come, so you have no excuse not to sing.

If you can’t get enough of this song, you’ll fit right in at Pegula Ice Arena, chanting a different version of this song to heckle the opposition’s goalie. Go wild.

Kernkraft 400 - Zombie Nation

It’s a huge misconception, but no, the name of the song is NOT Zombie Nation. Sorry to ruin it for you.

The iconic song that’s probably most affiliated with Penn State at this point has no lyrics, meaning you only need to know the part of the song you start chanting “We Are Penn State!”

This German techno jam from the nineties started out as a club song and was brought over to Beaver Stadium around 2005 — and it isn't going anywhere anytime soon.

Dreams and Nightmares - Meek Mill

This one’s for you, Philly.

Just about three hours from the original capital of the United States, it makes sense that this song can be heard at Penn State football games. With a solid chunk of Beaver Stadium being Philadelphia sports fans on any given Saturday, don’t be surprised when everyone around you knows every word to this song.

Don’t be the odd one out.

title: Former Penn State women's basketball assistant coach Amari Boyd hired at Denver
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another piece of Carolyn Kieger's old coaching staff has found a new home.

Former assistant coach Amari Boyd was hired at Denver in the same role, being announced as the last new coach on Tuesday.

Boyd has been with the Lady Lions since 2017, serving in various roles. She began her tenure as a student manager, before becoming an operations assistant and graduate assistant.

Before leaving Happy Valley for the Pioneers, Boyd served as the Director of Recruiting and Basketball Operations for the Lady Lions.

title: Column | Everything wrong with new ‘Enhanced Games'
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
The United States feels like a vacuum these days, and not even my comforting sports bubble is safe from the sideshow.

News is bubbling about a new sports game called Enhanced Games. This so‑called competition is essentially a chemical playground, where athletes can sample performance‑boosting options off the organizer’s “menu” in Las Vegas. (Well, where else would you set up such a drug-filled spectacle?)

If that doesn’t sound like a steroid speakeasy, I don’t know what does.

How far have we fallen when this is what society chooses to spotlight? I fully expect this thing to crash and burn. And so far, it’s right on track to do so, no matter how the PR team tries to spin it.

Even so, the fact that it even made it out of a boardroom and onto TV screens is enough to make me question everything I thought I knew.

Look at the figurehead, Bryan Johnson. He appeared on TV with Emmanuel Acho, dressed head‑to‑toe in black, sunglasses on, twirling an umbrella under the blazing Nevada sun.

Every inch of this operation screams the word wrong, and, frankly, it has no business existing. Johnson is unavoidable in all this, and it’s fair to say his image alone raises serious questions. Throughout his time in the public eye, Johnson has been adamant about his goal to reverse his body’s aging process; these games feel like another part of that.

Obsessed biohacking billionaires can indirectly use these participating athletes as their guinea pigs to test all sorts of substances. Either way, these organizers themselves are probably pumped with enough chemicals to sterilize a McDonalds, but they shouldn’t push this insane way of living onto others.

Sports are supposed to be about raw, unfiltered talent, which takes decades to refine and maximize. Flooding that with a pharmacy’s worth of chemicals makes about as much sense as putting premium gas in a lemon and expecting to win a race. These substances belong in the trainer’s room for recovery, not as a ticket to shove the human body into the danger zone.

Breaking records is clearly the main purpose of these events, as the founder told Vanity Fair he was downright upset over the lack of shattered figures. Now he’s dangling $10 million in front of anyone who can outrun Usain Bolt’s 100M record. These people don't care if the stands are empty, it’s all about rewriting history by trying to show that these chemicals are the secret piece sports have been missing.

We shouldn’t stand for it. Anyone with a pulse needs to call this what it is: a sham peddling an unhealthy lifestyle dressed up as progress. It feels like an offshoot of the polarizing looksmaxxing trend that has warped how young adults see themselves. It’s just another branch of that same urge to biohack your body in unproven, risky ways.

If the money flying around this thing doesn’t set off alarm bells, nothing will. The CEO, Maximilian Martin, is a finance guy with a degree from Wharton and not a hint of sports pedigree in sight. Sure, sports should be open to everyone, but they shouldn’t be hijacked by an outsider who thinks he can buy his way into rewriting the rulebook.

Even their website tagline is a warning sign: “Transforming human potential into superhumanity.” It sounds like the premise of a RoboCop knockoff, not a sports league, and goes against the very nature of what athletics is supposed to stand for. They can slap an athletics label on it all they want, but trusting these folks is at your own peril.

They’re dressing it up as some grand gift to humanity, calling it a scientific leap forward and trying to make the whole thing sound less taboo, but these are banned substances for a reason. No amount of ethical dressing can hide what’s really going on or convince us that this is somehow a genuine benefit to anyone.

Their real agenda becomes clear when you visit their website. The first thing you see is a link to their products that are “designed to support strength, energy, and longevity.”

That’s the real scam: to get their brand plastered everywhere while society is obsessed with looks and performance. Cash in, then disappear, using America’s love of sports as bait.

It’s rotten to the core and carefully engineered, and that’s why the founder is so desperate to see Bolt’s record fall. If it happens, there’ll always be an asterisk in the record books, and their product will be forever glued to it.

It’s a branding scheme trying to sneak a dangerous idea into the mainstream, and I can’t help but root for its failure.

title: Off the record | An old problem is entering the villa
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a fourth-year Collegian staffer under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic can do so through this anonymous form.

Dear Reader,

Hot new bombshells are entering the villa, and I’m definitely ready. Love Island USA premieres June 2 on Peacock, with an episode airing every day but Wednesday for the next six weeks. Huge news for the unemployed!

I’m kidding, I’m kidding. But for real, watching it feels like a new low — even if there’s nothing I love more in the summer than people on my TV smacking their lips while munching on avocado toast every morning.

The series has produced reality star royalty and iconic moments that will cycle through social media over and over again for the coming months.

Maybe you’re thinking: What redeeming qualities does Love Island have that I can take into my summer?

And the answer is: nothing.

There’s something so compelling about watching beautiful singles fight for the attention of a man who doesn’t care if she lives or dies. It’s like a trainwreck; I can’t look away. As much as I want to be one of those pretentious people who can’t be bothered with reality TV, let alone one where they spit milk into each other's mouths (sigh, I know), that’s just not me.

If you tune in this summer, which I still highly advise, the first thing you’ll probably notice is the contestants are constantly in swimwear. And if it’s on Shein, they’re in it.

While being in such a vulnerable position is something I don’t envy, I do think there’s a bigger takeaway. (Yes, I'm going to try to tie Love Island to a larger societal issue. Just stick with me.)

Love Island exemplifies the crucifixion of women’s bodies and emotional vulnerability that we simply don’t see in their male counterparts.

I can’t count the number of times I’ve seen women shamed for becoming “too attached” to their couple partner, called fat in their swimsuit or called “crazy” or “psycho” for simply playing the game.

Misogyny seeps into every aspect of life, and Love Island is no exception.

The show, while inherently designed to be sexual in nature, thrives on exposing women’s past dating history, body counts and physical appearances, yet turns a blind eye to men’s behaviors of the same calibre. When the men are actively exploring, making out and being deceitful, they quickly become America’s favorites.

As I said earlier, the dress code for the women in the villa is thongs and body-tight dresses, and the men wear 3-inch shorts with a buttoned shirt that ironically never seems to be buttoned at all. Strutting around with rock-hard abs and fake breast implants, the contestants set unrealistic body expectations for an audience that is notorious for struggling with body image.

Society needs to recognize that this isn’t the norm, and it’s taken weekly botox injections and unhealthy habits to achieve the bodies that are so highly praised. If you don’t have the same proportions as the hottest new bombshell, know you aren’t any less beautiful or worthy of love.

You’re probably thinking, “it’s never that serious,” but the truth is… it is.

The standards set by “celebrity” culture — yes, even Love Island’s pseudo-celebs — enable harmful behavior and enforce bigger issues of body dysphoria and disordered eating.

Your body is beautiful the way it is, and doesn't need to be compared against others. It certainly doesn’t need to be put up against women who’ve modified their bodies in ways unattainable to the normal person.

Of course, have fun watching. But remember your worth consists of so much more than your physical appearance.

Until next time,

Miss Behave

title: Promoting Penn State men’s basketball’s Brent Scott to AHC was an ‘easy decision’ for Mike Rhoades
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
When Jamal Brunt left for an assistant coaching job on the staff at Syracuse, Penn State had an opening in its associate head coach position. Mike Rhoades decided to stay in house for the replacement, elevating a long-time assistant of his.

Brent Scott was promoted to associate head coach this month after three years as an assistant coach at Penn State, developing players in the frontcourt for the team. He’s been on Rhoades’ staff since the 2016-17 season when they were at Rice, and Scott said he’s enjoyed being on the Nittany Lions’ staff.

“Super blessed and thankful for the opportunity,” Scott told The Daily Collegian on Friday. “It's been great being here at Penn State, being from Michigan. I grew up in Big Ten country, so being able to come back close to home and coaching in the best conference in the country has been awesome.”

Scott’s role at Penn State is to coach the team’s centers. For each of his three seasons on staff, the Nittany Lions have had a standout player at the position.

Most notably, Scott played a key role in developing Yanic Konan Niederhauser, who became the program’s first-ever Round 1 NBA draft pick in 2025. Konan Niederhauser spent the 2024-25 season with Penn State, averaging 12.9 points and 6.3 rebounds per game to become an All-Big Ten honorable mention.

Qudus Wahab was another center who received an All-Big Ten honorable mention, averaging 9.8 points and 7.8 rebounds per game under Scott’s tutelage in 2023-24. Even this past season, Scott guided center Ivan Jurić to average 10.2 points and 5.3 rebounds per game as a freshman.

Scott, who played professionally in the USBL and overseas in Europe, attributed his coaching ability to his playing days. He credited his position coach from his days of college ball at Rice, Tom Billeter.

“A lot of things he taught me I use today,” Scott said. “It's just taking time and developing. Biggest thing for me, as I'm a relationship-based guy, is getting to know guys, what they like, what their strengths are, what their weaknesses are.”

Scott will enter his 11th year working alongside Rhoades, having also been a part of his staff at VCU from 2017-23. Even outside of coaching, Scott said Rhoades is one of his best friends, highlighting the strength of their relationship.

With Scott’s tenure as an assistant and the job he’s done developing centers, Rhoades called it both an easy decision and a no-brainer to promote him to associate head coach.

“Big B’s loyalty to our program, to the players, to the staff, is second to none,” Rhoades said. “His approach, his coaching and his leadership and the way he teaches, it's high level. The one thing about Big B is he wants to give and give and give, and he doesn't want an ounce of credit.”

Heading into the 2026-27 season, it seems it could be business as usual for Scott developing Penn State’s centers. He is “so excited” about having Jurić back for his sophomore year, saying he “wanted him back more than anything.”

Scott added that Jurić has the chance to be a “really, really special” player. With Scott developing Jurić, Rhoades has praise for his new associate head coach.

“I'm really excited,” Rhoades said. “He's absolutely been crushing it here this spring with all the things we got to get done here going in this summer. I'm really proud of him, very thankful for him, and I really like our staff and the group we have, and he has a lot to do with it.”

title: Former Penn State WR Bobby Engram on 2027 NFF College Football Hall of Fame ballot
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
One of Penn State's greats is still making headlines for his collegiate career.

Wide receiver Bobby Engram was included on the 2027 College Football Hall of Fame Ballot by the National Football Foundation (NFF) on Monday.

There were 80 players and nine coaches from the FBS named to the ballot with the final class announcement coming in early 2027.

Engram is the lone former Nittany Lion on the list and would be the third Nittany Lion from the undefeated 1994 team's offense to be inducted, following Kerry Collins and Ki-Jana Carter

Engram made a name for himself as Penn State's all-time leading receiver with 3,026 receiving yards over the course of his career. He was also the first Nittany Lion in program history to post a 1,000-yard receiving season.

In 1994, Engram was the first-ever recipient of the Biletnikoff Award for the nation's top receiver, putting up 52 receptions for 1,029 yards.

By the end of his time in Happy Valley, Engram had set 12 total program records.

title: Former Penn State baseball player Ty Torti enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion is back in the portal.

Nova Southeastern infielder Ty Torti is entering the transfer portal as a graduate student, he announced Sunday via X.

Torti spent two seasons in Happy Valley from 2023-24, tallying a .355 batting average in 31 plate appearances. He then transferred to Nova Southeastern in the summer of 2024, where he's been ever since.

Torti played in 67 games across two seasons with the Sharks, who compete at the Division II level. He made 44 hits and had 24 RBIs during his time with Nova Southeastern.

He will now search for his third collegiate team of his career with one season of eligibility remaining.

title: Way-too-early 2027 NFL Draft predictions for Penn State players
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a 2026 NFL Draft where eight former Penn State players were drafted, projections and predictions are already beginning for the highly anticipated 2027 draft class.

While many Nittany Lions will be playing in the blue and white for the first time, there’s still enough talent on the team to begin projecting draft stock — with plenty of room for both growth and decline.

Here’s a look at where some Penn Staters could get selected during next year’s draft in Washington D.C.

Marcus Neal Jr., safety, Round 2

As of right now, the first player likely to hear his name called is safety Marcus Neal Jr., as the junior was one of the leading tacklers for Iowa State last season with 77. He also had a sack and two interceptions.

Neal is a player who’ll be all over the field in 2026 for the Nittany Lions and can make plays at any level. With safeties becoming such versatile assets, there has been a newfound importance of drafting safeties in early rounds, with multiple safeties going in the first round the past couple of years.

Penn State has had a lot of recent success with safeties getting drafted into the pros, including Zakee Wheatley, Jaylen Reed, Kevin Winston Jr. and Ji’Ayir Brown. Neal could join those names next year as an early selection, and barring a breakout from one of his teammates, he very well could be the first Nittany Lion off the board.

Tony Rojas, linebacker, Round 3

Tony Rojas’ first few games back will give more indication as to where he could be drafted, considering he’s coming off a torn ACL. But the intangibles Rojas brings will surely allow him to rise up the board.

Rojas’ on-field talent and athleticism allows him to fully play the linebacker position both as a run stopper and in pass coverage. No matter where Rojas’ draft stock is in January, it’s likely to continue rising during the pre-draft process.

The redshirt junior can absolutely fly around the field and could be in major contention to set records for a 40-yard dash time as a linebacker. It wouldn’t be surprising if Rojas hits low 4.40s or even into the 4.30s at the NFL Scouting Combine or Penn State’s Pro Day.

Audavion Collins, cornerback, Day 2-3

Another player whose stock will naturally rise because of his speed is Audavion Collins. But it’s not just his speed that’ll push him up draft boards.

Collins is the likely top cornerback this upcoming season despite plenty of other talent at the position. The 2026 defensive back room could very well be one of the most talented units as a whole and as the leader Collins, could be in for a big year and a rise is in his draft stock.

While Collins’ coverage skills are strong and he’s incredibly fast, his size could be why he falls down the board. Collins is listed at 5-foot-11, 180 pounds, which isn’t a typical NFL body. However, multiple cornerbacks in the 2026 NFL Draft had similar builds and went early in the draft.

Rocco Becht, quarterback, Day 3

Rocco Becht’s stock is very uncertain, given his injuries from last year and a fresh start at Penn State.

Becht will enter his final year with some of the highest quarterback experience in the country. His on-field presence and leadership will certainly help his stock, and his strong accuracy could increase his chances of hearing his name called.

The comparisons to Sean Clifford, who drafted in the fifth round in 2023, have already begun for Becht. His numbers are eerily similar to the former Penn State quarterback, which puts his Day 3 projection in a good spot if those similarities remain.

Chase Sowell, wide receiver, Day 3

Likely the top receiver this upcoming season, Chase Sowell could be in for a big year under the same system he had last year.

Sowell was the No. 1 receiver at Iowa State last year, even with fellow transfer Brett Eskildsen outgaining him by 26 yards. With the talent that’s alongside Sowell, it’ll allow him to get one-on-one opportunities where he can showcase his own talents.

The redshirt senior received comparisons from his wide receiver coach Kashif Moore to that of 2026 NFL draftee Skyler Bell, who Moore coached and developed at UConn. If Moore can unleash that type of development in Sowell, the Nittany Lion could hear his name called even earlier.

Other names to watch

Penn State could have around eight players drafted again next year. Other names to watch include running back Carson Hansen, tight end Benjamin Brahmer, offensive lineman Anthony Donkoh and cornerback Zion Tracy.

Hansen will likely be in at least a duo, if not a trio, that’ll rotate playing time which could limit his production and lower his stock. Brahmer’s big frame and overall ability will likely get him drafted, while Donkoh’s consistency and versatility could boost his draft position.

Despite getting a lot of hype from Matt Campbell, Tracy’s stock will be hard to rise compared to other cornerbacks because of his nickel position. The nickel corner spot, albeit very impactful and important, isn’t a spot that teams often use higher draft picks on.

title: Column | Advice from a recent graduate
author: Emma Lessick | The Daily Collegian
description:
There’s something about graduating that suddenly makes me feel qualified to give life advice — despite the fact that only a few years ago, I was walking around campus in your shoes, an underclassman at the bottom of the food chain.

Regardless, in the spirit of growth (and mild delusion), here’s my completely unsolicited advice, most of which I had to learn the hard way. So, maybe just take a moment to hear me out.

First, romanticize your life. I'm serious. Put your headphones on, pretend you’re the main character and walk to class like you have somewhere important to be (even if it’s just a lecture you will half listen to). It doesn’t fix everything, but trying to enjoy the small things can make the day exponentially better. Also, it encourages you to enjoy the mundane, everyday things.

Go to office hours. Not because you’re an overachiever or because you have deeply intellectual questions, but because most professors are significantly more generous to students whose names they recognize. I don’t make the rules.

Also, stop taking 8 a.m. classes with the mindset of “I’ll just get over it.” You won’t. It will get you. There’s no version of you, no matter how motivated, rested or optimistic, that enjoys walking to class while the sun is still figuring out what it wants to do for the rest of the day.

Here’s another one. Canvas will humble you. Every time. You can have your entire week planned out, your life together, your notes color-coded and then suddenly it's 11:58 p.m. and you're submitting something you started only a little bit ago.

Those pesky notifications at whatever time of day or night always gave me cardiac arrhythmia in anticipation of whether it was a grade, message from my professor or a reminder for an assignment I’ve yet to turn in.

Now onto less academic stuff:

Text your friends back. Not eventually. Not “I’ll get to it later.” Later turns into never and then suddenly you're “so busy” and find your inner circle of friends shrinking by the day.

Take pictures of everything, even the random, boring moments. You think you’ll remember them. You won’t. At the end of your college years, your camera roll will end up meaning more to you than your GPA.

Learn how to be alone without feeling lonely. There will be nights when no one’s around, plans fall through or everyone else seems busy. Take yourself out. Go on a walk. Go get food. It’s not sad, it’s independence, self-care and a skill to be able to enjoy your own company.

Stop waiting to feel “put together.” No one wakes up one day and suddenly has their life perfectly aligned. Everyone is improvising. Some people just have better outfits while doing it (couldn’t be me).

Don’t stay in situations (or with people) that make you feel small. Whether it’s friendships, relationships or just the environment in general, if you constantly feel, or are being made to feel like you’re not enough, that’s your sign to leave. Protect your peace.

Likewise, let yourself outgrow people and places. It doesn’t have to mean anything dramatic happened. Sometimes you change, sometimes other people change, that’s allowed.

Say yes more than you say no, but know when to say no. Go out, try things, be spontaneous. But also, if you need a night in, take it. Missing one night out with your friends will not ruin your social life.

Drink water. Seriously. Not everything can be solved with iced coffee, vibes or alcohol. Stay hydrated with H2O.

Call your parents (or whoever feels like home). Even if it’s quick. Even if you think you don’t have much to say. One day, you’ll wish you did it more.

You will be OK. Even when things don’t go how you planned, even when you feel behind or have no idea what you’re doing next, you’ll figure it out. You always do.

If you take something from this, let it be this. College doesn’t end with some big moment where everything suddenly makes sense. It kind of just ends, and you're left with a camera roll full of blurry pictures and the absolute best memories.

And if all else fails, at least check Canvas before 11:59 p.m. Some habits are just worth keeping for a while.

title: How to celebrate Pride Month in State College
author: Saxtyn Lutz | The Daily Collegian
description:
During the month of June, communities across the world will be celebrating Pride Month, an observance and commemoration of LGBTQ+ culture.

State College hosts a variety of Pride events throughout June. Here are a few ways you can partake in the celebrations.

State College Pride Parade

Beginning at 10 a.m. on June 13, vehicles and community members will parade through the streets of downtown State College.

Vehicles begin at State College Area High School, will drive down Westerly Pikeway and then turn onto South Allen Street where marchers will follow them outside of the State College Municipal Building. Both vehicles and marchers will continue down South Allen Street, turn onto West College Avenue, then onto South Burrows Street and finally onto West Nittany Avenue.

From there, everyone will convene at the Sidney Friedman Park for the Pride Festival.

Those intending to participate in the parade can register here. Community members are also welcome to gather around the streets listed above to enjoy the parade.

State College Pride 2026 Festival

From noon to 4 p.m., a festival with live music, drag, community and vendors will be hosted by CentreLGBT+ Grand Marshal Mayor Erica Deuso of Downingtown, Pennsylvania.

Over the course of the festival, multiple performers will take to the stage, including Margot Bevington, Diego Man-Dana, Thomasin Grace, Porch Party, Eric Ian Farmer and Friends and Tempest Productions.

The festival will also include a variety of food vendors to satisfy summer-day cravings, including ice cream, lemonade and barbecue.

State College Pride After Party

The celebration doesn’t end once the festival is over. At 7 p.m., those who are 21+ can head to The Basement for an after party hosted by CentreLGBT+. This event will have specialty Pride drinks, photo-ops, music from DJ Timeless, dancing, community and celebration.

There is a $10 cover fee.

Youth Gayla

The Youth Gayla, hosted by CentreLGBT+, will be held from 7 p.m. to 10 p.m. on June 20 at the Millbrook Marsh Nature Center. It is a prom-style event that aims to provide a safe space for LGBTQ+ youth in grades 9 through 12.

Attendees can enjoy music, snacks and photo-ops for this masquerade-themed event. There is a $20 admission fee.

Pride Storefront Competition

Between June 1 and June 31, CentreLGBT+ is hosting a State College Pride Window Display Contest.

Select stores downtown will decorate their storefront windows with Pride-themed decorations, and people can vote for their favorite display by scanning the QR code on each respective window throughout the month.

Participating stores include Lions Pride, Chumley’s, Downtown State College Improvement District, the State Theatre, Connections, 3 Dots Downtown and Yoga Lab.

Pets & Pride Photo Contest

CentreLGBT+ is hosting a Pets & Pride virtual photo contest where participants can submit a photo of their pet.

The 2025 winner was a smiling Dalmatian named Paris who had rainbow spots painted on her back. The deadline to submit a photo is June 16 by 5 p.m. Only one entry is allowed per household.

More information on submissions can be found here.

Queer Meet-up

Every Saturday between 3 p.m. and 5 p.m., the Print Factory in Bellefonte hosts an event catered toward teens and young adults who are queer, questioning or allies.

title: Attending NSO, essential purchases | Items for first-years to check off their summer to-do lists
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 2026-27 academic year is right around the corner, and with it comes a new class of freshmen about to step foot on campus. College is exciting, but it can also feel overwhelming when you start tallying everything that needs to happen before move-in day.

Here are some important things to add to your summer to-do list before arriving at Penn State.

Attend NSO (New Student Orientation)

All first-year students are required to attend New Student Orientation (NSO). Here, students learn about what University Park offers students — and it’s also when you register for fall classes.

NSO has multiple days available throughout the summer. Students must complete the NSO tasklist, online academic consultation and course scheduling window, and attend an in-person NSO program.

File FAFSA

The Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) is a form from the U.S. Department of Education used by colleges to determine eligibility for federal, state and institutional financial aid. FAFSA is also used to determine grants and work-study funds.

The form must be filled out every year.

Submit Immunization Records

All incoming first-years are required to submit immunization records. There may also be additional vaccination requirements for those living on campus.

To submit immunization records, head to the myUHS portal.

Select housing

First-year students at University Park are required to live on campus.

To choose housing, sign in to the eLiving system with your Penn State Access ID and fill out the HFS Contract.

Students are allowed to select a preference — East, Pollock, South, West, or North Halls — for their dorm, but this preference is not guaranteed. Submitting the housing contract early does not increase the chance of getting preferred housing.

Penn State does not use a survey to match roommates, students are allowed to choose if they would like to room with someone they know. The roommate request must be mutual.

Set Up Mobile id+ Card

Penn State now requires students to use a Mobile id+ card instead of a physical one.

To set it up, students are required to submit a photo of themselves or a valid government-issued photo ID or valid passport. After the picture loads, download the Transact eAccounts app and log in through your Penn State account. Then, add the ID to your phone’s wallet app.

Select Meal Plan

Students must log in to the eLiving website and choose one of the three Campus Meal Plan options:

Level 1 - $987, total charge of $2,737

Level 2 - $1,238, total charge of $2,988

Level 3 - $1,468, total charge of $3,218

Football Lottery

Students interested in attending football games should submit a request for student tickets through the Penn State Student Account Manager during their assigned class-specific window. The window typically opens in late July and closes early August.

Everyone will have the same chance of getting tickets no matter when the request is submitted.

Essential dorm items

Comfort and decor

Mattress toppers are a great way to make those way-too-firm dorm beds more comfortable. Make sure it’ll fit a twin or twin XL, the size of campus dorm mattresses.

Students should also bring sheets, a comforter, pillows and blankets. Try picking out your favorite colors or designs to make your dorm feel a little more at-home.

Many dorms on campus — like West, Pollock and South — are unrenovated, so many rooms do not have proper air conditioning. Having a fan on standby helps when the summer heat is sweltering those first few months in the fall, and later on in the spring semester.

To feel more at home, having a small rug is a great addition to a dorm room. Not only does it make those tiled floors a bit more comfortable, but it’s a great way to add some extra decoration to your room.

Overhead lights can be quite annoying during those late-night study sessions when you’re trying not to wake your roommate. Having a desk lamp is helpful to contain that light to your side of the room. And some warmer lighting is always good for extra coziness.

Extra things to have on hand

Of course, it’s necessary to have things to clean your room. A small, hand-held vacuum and swiffer jet is easily stored, and they come in super handy during Sunday cleaning sessions. You can also bring wipes to clean door handles, desks and other areas of the room that collect dust.

As many of the bathrooms in East, Pollock, South, West or North Halls have communal bathrooms, having shower slippers would be beneficial.

Umbrellas are also helpful to shield you from rain on your way to class, or as you make your way downtown with your friends.

Having cold medicine is important as students are living close to one another. And there’s always the dreaded frat flu.

Reusable water bottles are also nice to bring with you, as you can keep it on your nightstand for those 3 a.m. chugs or with you as you attend class. Most dorm buildings have water fountains throughout.

title: Gavin McKenna, Jaxon Cover and Pierce Mbuyi set to perform at 2026 NHL Combine
author: Kippi Hall | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions are making one last roar before this year's NHL Draft.

Forward Gavin McKenna, along with recruits Jaxon Cover and Pierce Mbuyi, will be attending the 2026 NHL Combine, which will run from Monday, June 1 to Friday, June 6 in Buffalo, New York.

McKenna, a Whitehorse, Yukon, native, is Penn State's first Big Ten Scoring Champion with 38 points consisting of 11 goals and 27 assists. His 2025-26 season set the single-season program record for assists with 36.

The 2026 Hobey Baker Award Finalist entered his freshman season at Penn State rated as the the No. 1 prospect for the 2026 NHL Draft.

Cover, a left wing for the London Knights in the Ontario Hockey League (OHL), committed to Penn State in April toting 52 points, 20 goals and 32 assists for the 2025-26 season. The 4-star recruit played in 67 games for the Knights.

Mbuyi, another left wing from Mississauga, Ontario, was named OHL Rookie of the Year last season after achieving 52 points, 29 goals and 23 assists in 63 games for the Attack. The 5-star recruit committed to Penn State in June of last year.

title: Penn State track and field's Hayley Kitching breaks 2-day old school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After setting the 800-meter record during the NCAA East First Round prelims on Thursday, Hayley Kitching has done it again.

Kitching broke her record for the second time this week during the 800m finals, running 1:59.15 to finish third overall and book her ticket to nationals.

The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native ran 1:59.60 during the prelims on Thursday to win the race, which was a school record then.

Kitching marks the fourth Nittany Lion to qualify in the 800m for the NCAA Championships as she'll compete in her third outdoor championships during her career.

title: Penn State baseball adds Fairfield outfielder transfer TJ Schmalzle
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a disappointing year, the Nittany Lions entered the transfer portal to retool their lineup.

With key holes already opened up via graduation and portal departures, such as Jack Porter and Nate Voss, coach Mike Gambino added his second incoming bat.

After earning a commitment from East Stroudsburg University's Shanley Walls, Gambino took another swing on a lower-prestige conference star in the form of Fairfield's TJ Schmalzle. The Lake Wallenpaupack. Pennsylvania native posted over a .280 batting average in his last two starting seasons with the Stags and became a real ironman, appearing in over 50 games per year.

However, last season's senior year campaign was a breakout year for Schmalzle, with a career high 11 home runs and 42 RBIs. He also improved all aspects of his slash line except his on-base percentage, which took a slight hit with lessened walk and heightened strikeout rates.

Nevertheless, he adds needed stability to centerfield, with a perfect fielding percentage last year, he'll slot in to captain the outfield in an attempt to help the Nittany Lions have a bounce-back year.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's lacrosse midfielder Anna Salerno earns IWLCA Senior All-Star nod
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
A strong final collegiate season just got even better for midfielder Anna Salerno.

After four seasons at St. Mary's, the Bel Air, Maryland, native posted 29 points in 19 games in her graduate student year with the Nittany Lions, along with a team high three game winning goals.

After such a strong performance, Salerno earned second-team All-Big Ten honors. Still, the accolades continued as she is now set to participate in the IWLCA Senior All-Star Game for the Blue Team, led by John Hopkins coach Tim McCormack.

Salerno's participation in the 2026 All-Star Game marks another consecutive year that a blue and white player has been invited to the competition, after Ellie Hollin and Lauren Saltz earned the honor in 2025.

After being unselected in the latest Women's Lacrosse League draft, this game gives Salerno one last chance to compete against the best of the best and showcase her talents for the next level.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey adds DIII goalie transfer Savanna Muir
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions have made an addition between the pipes.

After program icon Katie DeSa finished her senior season, and Lauren Hernick entered the transfer portal, the blue and white have made an addition to the position group after some big losses.

Savanna Muir arrives from SUNY Cortland, where she saw no action over two years on the roster, yet the Elizabeth, Pennsylvania, native has a chance to return to form in her home state after a stellar career with the Pittsburgh Penguins Elite Youth teams.

Muir also saw appearances this year with the Bishop Kearney Selects in Rochester, New York, as a part of the 19U AAA U.S. Nationals. This season with the BK Selects, she compiled a 9-1 record over 11 starts, with 6 shutouts.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field's Jake Palermo sets 400m school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the first time in over 50 years, Penn State has a new 400-meter school record holder.

Sophomore Jake Palermo set the record in the 400m finals at the NCAA East First Round, running 45.19 seconds to finish 14th in the race.

Palermo previously tied for the No. 2 mark in school history with a time of 45.22 seconds, but took it a step further on Friday, marking the third school record at the qualifier meet.

While the Rochester, New York, native missed out on an NCAA Championship qualification by two places, he still broke a 51-year-old record at Penn State, last set by Mike Sands in 1975.

title: Penn State women's volleyball announces full 2026 schedule
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is taking its talents primetime.

The Nittany Lions’ full nonconference and Big Ten schedule was released on Thursday. The slate features three neutral-site games and the inaugural conference tournament at the end of the year.

The year starts with the previously announced Spikes Under The Lights exhibition tournament at AT&T Stadium, before a three-match homestand at the Penn State Invitational to begin the year. Making the trip to Happy Valley will be UConn, High Point and Hofstra.

The first match away from Happy Valley for the blue and white will be in the historic Wrigley Field against Kentucky, which defeated Penn State last year. The Nittany Lions will stay in Chicago to face DePaul before heading back to the state to face Stanford at The Palestra, as the venue celebrates its centennial anniversary in 2026.

The Nittany Lions' four remaining out-of-conference games are highlighted by the Penn State Classic, where Tennessee and USF visit. The weekend classic was originally first confirmed as a part of the Volunteers’ schedule release.

Big Ten play begins with an away clash against Michigan on Sept. 25, before also taking on the Spartans two days later.

Nebraska marks the Nittany Lions’ first home conference opponent on Oct. 1, to kickoff a four-match home series.

Finishing out the highlights for the schedule will be the Big Ten Tournament from Nov. 20 to 26 at Fishers Event Center in Fishers, Indiana.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘Pay it forward’ | Penn State men’s basketball hosts annual Coaches vs. Cancer Golf Tournament
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
New and old faces of the Penn State men’s basketball community gathered in Walker Clubhouse at Penn State’s golf courses on Friday. Two rounds of golf were played with many members catching up with each other, all in support of a cause that dates back three decades for the team.

It was Penn State’s 29th-annual golf tournament for its chapter of Coaches vs. Cancer, a program that has the National Association of Basketball Coaches help the American Cancer Society defeat cancer. The Nittany Lions’ chapter recently passed $5 million total raised for the cause all time, highlighting the work Penn State has done.

“The way I was raised, we talk about all the time, is to pay it forward,” Mike Rhoades said. “You have these great opportunities in your life, for us sometimes around basketball, and you get these great opportunities and moments of your lives that come your way. You pay that back by helping others and serving others.”

The Penn State chapter of Coaches vs. Cancer was started in 1995 by former head coach Bruce Parkhill. The chapter’s annual golf tournament began two years later in 1997 by then-head coach Jerry Dunn, and the tradition has carried on to today.

Within Penn State’s chapter fundraising, the golf tournament has raised over $3.2 million to date, according to a recent news release. Over 425 golfers participated, and Rhoades said it’s been a lot of fun to meet everyone who shows up.

“Coach Parkhill and coach Dunn got this thing rolling, and usually great things in life start with people coming together with the ideas, and then following through on those ideas and getting people on board to make that impact,” Rhoades said. “Coach Parkhill started that, and the neat thing about all this is, they still to this day feel such a good part of it, and that's why they come back. Their families come back, former players come back, and that tells you a lot about those men.”

Program alum and former assistant coach Joe Crispin was back at Penn State for the golf tournament after becoming the head coach at Air Force earlier this offseason. He said the event has “always been an inspirational thing and a unification thing for the community” and he wants to come back for it every time he can.

The field of players also included brothers Dwayne Stephens and Jarrett Stephens. Dwayne was recently hired as a new assistant coach for the staff while Jarrett is an alum who played from 1995-2000.

“It's an event that's even more special to me. Jarrett and myself, our mom is a cancer survivor, so I know firsthand how it impacts a lot of people's lives and families,” Dwayne said. “Anytime I can do something or be a part of something that's going to benefit and help cancer, I'm all in.”

In addition to the golf tournament, festivities for Penn State’s Coaches vs. Cancer chapter this week included a reception Thursday night. There’s also an online auction happening that gives all proceeds to the chapter, including bidding for a dinner with Rhoades.

Through all the donations and events, Penn State’s chapter recently became the fourth Coaches vs. Cancer chapter to raise over $5 million.

“(It) says a lot about Penn State, our community here, and State College, and so many people being a part of it and impacted in a special way,” Rhoades said.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: 40 year anniversary special | Top plays, moments from 1986 national championship season
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State was dominant throughout the 1986 season, allowing it to play for the national title against Miami, in which it’d come out on top.

However, the Nittany Lions wouldn’t have been in the title game without specific moments throughout the season which showcased a championship-minded team that wound up standing atop the college football pedestal.

Here are some of the top plays and moments from the 1986 national championship season.

Early season test, victory vs. Cincinnati

Penn State began the season 4-0 and weren’t tested at all up to that point. Its closest victory came in a road win over Boston College by 11 points in Week 2, but the Nittany Lions team blew out their other opponents. However, that wasn’t the case in a Week 5 matchup against Cincinnati.

Late in the fourth quarter, the Nittany Lions trailed the Bearcats 17-14 but were driving down the field. After a short run by running back Steve Smith, the team lined up in i-formation from just outside the 5-yard line.

Quarterback John Shaffer handed it off to power back David Clark, who fought and plowed his way through defenders before carrying multiple Bearcats into the end zone, giving Penn State the 21-17 lead.

Despite Penn State’s defense getting picked apart throughout the game by Cincinnati quarterback Danny McCoin, the Nittany Lions held off the Bearcats for the final minutes, escaping with a 23-17 victory.

Statement win over No. 2 Alabama

Ranked No. 6 in the country, Penn State entered Tuscaloosa, Alabama with its first major test of the season.

Despite being ranked lower than the Crimson Tide, the Nittany Lions appeared to be the better team throughout the game, winning 23-3 while scoring the final 23 points of the game.

Penn State was led by two rushing touchdowns from D.J. Dozier and Blair Thomas, respectively, as the offensive line mauled a star-studded Alabama front seven. On the defensive end, the Nittany Lions shut down an offensive led by quarterback Mike Shula and running back Bobby Humphrey.

This game put the nation on notice that Penn State was a legitimate contender for the national title after traveling into Alabama and taking down the then-No. 2 Crimson Tide. The Nittany Lions took over that No. 2 for the next few weeks.

2-point conversion stop vs. Maryland

In another tight battle, Penn State needed a victory against a Maryland team who was 4-4 at the time to keep any title hopes alive.

Leading 17-9, the Nittany Lions needed one final stop to fend off a Maryland team who had an upset on its mind. The Terrapins marched down the field and scored a touchdown to pull to within two points of Penn State.

During a time period when ties could be the result of a game, a tie against Maryland could’ve been detrimental to championship hopes. But the Terrapins were still in need of a 2-point conversion to tie the game up.

Maryland lined up for a 2-point conversion with hopes of putting a damper on Penn State’s title run. It wouldn’t succeed, as defensive back Duffy Cobbs batted the ball down and the Nittany Lions came out on top 17-15, maintaining their perfect 9-0 record.

Gary Wilkerson’s game-saving hit vs. Notre Dame

Two weeks later, Penn State traveled to South Bend to take on rival Notre Dame. It was the first year under Lou Holtz, and while the Fighting Irish weren't necessarily having a strong season, they still showed signs of talent.

Those signs all came together to battle the No. 3 Nittany Lions in a tight game. Penn State was on the receiving end of some major breaks as a kickoff return touchdown from eventual 1987 Heisman Trophy winner Tim Brown was called back for a penalty. Brown also dropped a third-down pass later in the game that ended up being costly in Notre Dame’s upset hopes.

Despite this, Notre Dame still had a chance to win the game at the end of the fourth quarter. With third-and-goal from the 18-yard line, Notre Dame quarterback Steve Beuerlein dropped back and fired a pass to tight end Joel Williams in the end zone. But Penn State cornerback Gary Wilkerson popped Williams, breaking up the pass and saving the game for the Nittany Lions.

The fourth down play was completed but well short of the end zone, and Penn State held on to defeat Notre Dame, 24-19.

Fiesta Bowl victory versus Miami

After the victory over Notre Dame, it was smooth sailing to the Fiesta Bowl, where the Nittany Lions met a star-studded Miami team.

Led by 1986 Heisman Trophy winner Vinny Testaverde, Penn State had its hands full. However, the Nittany Lions took that underdog mentality to the Hurricanes.

Penn State’s defense completely shut down the nation’s top quarterback, as Testaverde threw five interceptions, none more important than the final one to close the game out.

With 18 seconds remaining, Testaverde had a chance to redeem his four picks from earlier in the game. His fourth down pass was thrown toward multiple blue jerseys and was picked off in the end zone by Pete Giftopoulos.

Penn State’s fifth interception of Testaverde sealed the deal of what was perceived to be an upset of the previously dominant Hurricane team. Penn State became the 1986 national champions with a 14-10 win over Miami.

MORE 40 YEAR ANNIVERSARY CONTENT

title: Centre County news outlets sue Penn State Board of Trustees over alleged First Amendment violations
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Centre County news outlets are suing the Penn State Board of Trustees over alleged First Amendment violations due to updated bylaws which they allege control what trustees say to the press and public.

The lawsuit was filed on Wednesday in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania by Spotlight PA, the Centre Daily Times and StateCollege.com.

The lawsuit alleges that bylaw amendments adopted by the Board of Trustees in November 2025 “effectively eliminated trustees’ freedom to communicate with Plaintiffs” and “severely restrict trustees’ speech rights.”

Trustee Code of Conduct Section 2.03 (c) of the amended bylaws, state “negative or critical public statements about the Board, the University or its students, alumni, community, faculty, staff, and other stakeholders do not serve the University’s interests and are inconsistent with a Trustee’s fiduciary obligation to act always in the best interests of the University.”

The lawsuit alleges this constitutes a “gag policy” and highly restricts trustee speech.

Additionally, the amended policies restrict trustees’ speech to the press by requiring them to seek and obtain advance approval of statements to the media, effectively allowing Board leadership to “pick and choose” what information and opinions are shared, according to the lawsuit.

Trustees are also subject to disciplinary action for going against these policies, according to the bylaws.

The lawsuit in turn alleges that the “Gag Policy” “unlawfully infringes trustees" First Amendment rights as speakers, and, in turn, infringes the First Amendment rights of Plaintiffs to receive such information from trustees who are "willing to speak" about Board matters but are "restrained from doing so."

Wyatt DuBois, a university spokesperson, said in an email to the Collegian that “the University generally does not comment on pending litigation.”

The Board of Trustees is not new to lawsuits regarding free speech, having been sued multiple times in the past few years by at least three plaintiffs. The Board was sued by former trustee Barry Fenchak in 2024, alleging defamation and retaliation.

Later in 2024, trustee Anthony Lubrano sued the Board, also alleging retaliation for comments made to the media regarding the renaming of Penn State’s Beaver Stadium.

Spotlight PA, who is a plaintiff in the current lawsuit against the Board, previously sued the Board in 2023 for violations of Pennsylvania’s Sunshine Act. The Sunshine Act requires publication agencies to act and deliberate in public meetings that are open to the general public.

This case was settled in June 2025 and the Board was required to disclose more information about closed-door meetings and complete trainings on open meetings law.
